steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question. The blank must follow logically from the archaeological evidence: clam size and abundance both increased during the same period when the Kwakwaka'wakw terraced garden method was in use. Any correct conclusion must stay within what the evidence actually shows—no unsupported comparisons or claims about other groups or present-day practices.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘the practice used by ancestors of modern Kwakwaka'wakw people not only effectively maintained a food source but also promoted its robustness.’: Clam abundance increasing shows the food source was maintained; clam size also increasing shows it became more robust. This follows directly from the evidence. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘non-Indigenous people around the Pacific Northwest coast adopted the practice developed by the Kwakwaka'wakw people after observing its efficacy.’: The passage never mentions non-Indigenous adoption of the practice. Inferring this from evidence about clam size and abundance alone is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘there is greater corroboration in the archaeological record of ecological practices described in Kwakwaka'wakw songs than of those described in Sakha and Kaluli songs.’: The Sakha and Kaluli songs are mentioned only as background examples; no archaeological evidence is presented for them, so no comparison is possible. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘although contemporary Kwakwaka'wakw people have a deep understanding of and appreciation for the fishing and farming practices used by their ancestors, they no longer implement those methods.’: The evidence concerns past clam abundance; it says nothing about whether modern Kwakwaka'wakw people currently use or have abandoned these methods. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
